caption.text:
4,000 years old. Not a single word has been read.

The Indus Valley script survives on nearly 5,000 seals across one of the world's oldest civilizations. Roughly 400 distinct signs, arranged with mathematical precision — entropy analysis and AI confirm it encodes real information. Yet after more than a century, no sentence has been reliably translated.

Iravatham Mahadevan, a Tamil civil servant, gave up his career to spend 37 years building the only complete concordance of these signs. He died in 2018 at age 88, his life's work unfinished. The Tamil Nadu government has now offered $1 million to anyone who can decipher it. The original seals sit in the National Museum in Delhi, the Archaeological Survey of India, and the Roja Muthiah Research Library in Chennai — waiting.

We know the script is not random. We do not know what it says.

What would you risk to solve history's oldest unread message?

text.line_2: Stored in Delhi, Chennai, and beyond.
Died without cracking a single word.
Still unclaimed. What does it say?
